title: Memory Management
summary: Understand garbage collection, memory leaks, weak references, and use TypeScript patterns to write memory-efficient code

sections.title: Memory Management Is Like Organizing a Warehouse
sections.content: Your program's memory is like a warehouse. Every time you create an object (a new box), it takes up shelf space. JavaScript's garbage collector is the cleanup crew that looks for boxes no longer reachable — objects that nothing references anymore — and reclaims that shelf space. The key insight is: an object is 'alive' as long as there is a reference to it somewhere. If you accidentally keep references (like storing DOM elements in a global array), those objects never get cleaned up, causing a 'memory leak' — like shelves filling up with boxes labeled 'maybe useful someday' that never get thrown away. TypeScript cannot prevent memory leaks at compile time, but good patterns can.

sections.title: Memory Leak via Accumulating Event Listeners
sections.content: Demonstrates a common memory leak pattern and how to fix it with proper cleanup

sections.title: WeakRef, WeakMap, and WeakSet — Letting Go Gracefully
sections.content: Standard Map and Set hold strong references, preventing garbage collection of their keys and values. WeakMap and WeakSet hold 'weak' references — if the only reference to an object is through a WeakMap key, the garbage collector can still reclaim it. This is useful for caching, metadata, or private data attached to objects without preventing their cleanup. Imagine a library that stamps each borrowed book with a due date using invisible ink — when the book is returned and destroyed, the stamp disappears automatically. WeakMap keys must be objects (not primitives). Use WeakMap for storing per-instance private data in classes or caching computed results tied to object lifetimes.

sections.title: Creating a Circular Reference That Prevents Garbage Collection
sections.content: The #1 advanced mistake is creating circular references between objects that prevent cleanup, especially in older JavaScript engines (modern engines handle simple cycles fine). Wrong approach: 'parent.children = [child]; child.parent = parent;' — in engines with mark-and-sweep GC this is fine, but the real issue is leaking the root reference. The actual danger is not cycles but forgetting to nullify references in global caches or long-lived objects. Correct: always clean up references to objects you no longer need — set to null, remove from arrays, delete from Maps. If you cache objects, implement a maximum size or TTL (time-to-live) policy.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A real-time analytics system tracks user sessions. Each session object is stored in a Map<string, Session> keyed by session ID. Sessions should be automatically removed after 30 minutes of inactivity. Implement a 'SessionManager' that: (1) uses a Map to store sessions, (2) has a method 'getOrCreate(sessionId: string)' that creates a new session or returns existing one while updating a 'lastAccessed' timestamp, and (3) has a cleanup method that removes sessions where 'Date.now() - lastAccessed > 1800000'. Bonus: discuss whether WeakMap would be appropriate here.
hints: Why would a WeakMap not work for this scenario — what kind of keys does it require?
How does the cleanup method iterate and remove expired entries safely?
What would happen if two operations tried to create the same session ID simultaneously?
